reasoning: The 4-week moving average (MA) of continued claims has seen a sharp downward trajectory in the past month, falling from 1,952,500 in early November to 1,887,750 by December 20. Because this is a 4-week MA, the value on December 27 will replace the data from 4 weeks prior (the week of November 29, which was part of a much higher average level). For the MA to increase on December 27, the raw weekly claims would have to spike significantly above their recent run rate, which is highly unlikely given the strong negative momentum. Therefore, the chance of an increase by Dec 28 is very low (15%). Looking ahead to Jan 20, the downward momentum may stabilize, but historical data from the same period last year shows continued claims hovered comfortably around a lower equilibrium (~1,860,000) throughout the winter, suggesting staying below current levels (35%). By March 21, general uncertainty increases and broader macroeconomic trends (like labor market softening) could take over, pulling the probability closer to a coin toss (50%).
